steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.Nihilne: 'Nihilne' is a pronoun + enclitic particle: 'nihil' (nothing) + '-ne' (question marker)

hints.to die: 'to die' is 'mori' — a deponent verb, not related to 'movit'
hints.to change: 'to change' is 'mutare' — 'movit' means to move or disturb

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'movet' — 'movit' with the -it perfect ending is perfect tense
hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'movebat' — 'movit' is perfect indicative

hints.moneo, monere, monui, monitum: These are the parts of 'monere' (to warn) — similar spelling but different verb
hints.morior, mori, mortuus sum: These are the parts of 'mori' (to die) — a deponent verb
hints.muto, mutare, mutavi, mutatum: These are the parts of 'mutare' (to change) — a 1st conjugation verb

hints.Accusative: Accusative singular of neuter 'praesidium' looks the same, but here it is the subject that should have moved Catiline
hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'praesidii' — 'praesidium' is nominative, the subject of 'movit'
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'praesidio' — 'praesidium' is nominative here

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'praesidium' with -ium ending is 2nd declension neuter
hints.3rd: 3rd declension neuters have varied endings — 'praesidium' is a standard 2nd declension -ium neuter
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns end in -us/-u — 'praesidium' is 2nd declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'Palatium' — 'Palati' with the -i ending is genitive singular
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'Palatio' — 'Palati' is the genitive form
hints.Locative: Locative is possible for place names, but 'Palati' here modifies 'praesidium' as a genitive

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'tu' — 'te' is the accusative/ablative form, here accusative as object of 'movit'
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'tibi' — 'te' is accusative here
hints.Ablative: 'te' can be ablative, but here it's the direct object of 'movit' (accusative)

steps.prompt: What does the enclitic '-ne' on 'Nihilne' signal?

hints.Negation: 'nihil' already means 'nothing' — the '-ne' is an interrogative enclitic, not a negation
hints.Emphasis: While emphatic in tone, '-ne' specifically marks the sentence as a direct question
hints.A relative clause: Relative clauses use 'qui/quae/quod' — '-ne' introduces a direct question

translation: Did not the nightly guard of the Palatine move you at all?
